content:
Python

Artificial Intelligence

Software Engineering

Chatbots

Web Development

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Have you ever stood in a massive queue at your university food court, frantically checking your watch, wondering if you’ll make it to your next class? At Lovely Professional University (LPU), with thousands of students and over 60 distinct canteens scattered across the campus, the lunch rush is nothing short of chaotic.
I decided to solve this problem by building the LPU Smart Food Pre-Booking System — an end-to-end, AI-powered WhatsApp chatbot and vendor dashboard that completely eliminates the queue.
Here is a deep dive into the architecture, the technical challenges, and the senior-level optimizations I implemented to make this a production-ready product.

The Vision
The goal was simple, but the execution was complex:

• The Student Experience: A student texts a dedicated WhatsApp number: “Get me 2 veg burgers and a cold coffee at 2 PM”. The system instantly parses the intent, checks the database, confirms the price, and schedules the order.

• The Vendor Experience: The canteen vendor has a live Kanban-style dashboard on their tablet. When an order arrives, it pops up in the “Incoming” column. When the food is cooked, they click “Mark as Ready,” which triggers an automated WhatsApp message back to the student’s phone.

The Tech Stack

• Backend: Python, FastAPI

• Database: SQLite with SQLAlchemy ORM

• Frontend: HTML5, CSS3, Vanilla JavaScript

• AI/NLP: Google GenAI SDK (Gemini 1.5 Pro)

• Messaging: Twilio WhatsApp API

Challenge 1: The NLP Engine & The “Indestructible” Fallback
Building a chatbot that forces users to type exact commands (e.g., ORDER ID: 5) is a terrible user experience. I wanted students to talk naturally.
To achieve this, I integrated Google Gemini 1.5 Pro. The LLM reads the raw WhatsApp message, extracts the food items, calculates quantities, and returns a strict JSON payload representing the user’s intent (order, cancel, track, or view_menu).
But what if the API fails? API rate limits and network timeouts are real. To make the system bulletproof, I wrote a robust Regex Fallback Parser in Python. If Gemini fails to respond within the timeout window, the fallback parser kicks in. It dynamically splits sentences by commas and conjunctions, strips string noise, and extracts item names and quantities using raw regex mapping. The student never even notices the switch.

Challenge 2: Taming the Data Swamp (1,032 Items, 65 Restaurants)
The university provided the menu data in completely unstructured Excel files. One file had standard columns, while another used nested headers where the “Category” acted as a row title spanning across the sheet.
I wrote a custom Python ingestion pipeline using pandas and openpyxl. The script dynamically crawled through 63 separate sheets, correctly mapped the unformatted headers, appended category metadata to the item names, and seeded everything into a clean, relational SQLite database.
The result? A massive, highly queryable database containing exactly 1,032 menu items across 65 distinct restaurants.

Challenge 3: Algorithmic Optimization & Senior-Level Refactoring
As the dataset grew to 65 restaurants, testing revealed a critical edge case: Duplicate Pricing. If a student ordered a “Veg Biryani”, the system realized that 10 different restaurants sold “Veg Biryani”. It accidentally added the price of all 10 biryanis to the single order!
To fix this, I overhauled the architecture and implemented several senior-level optimizations:
1. N+1 Query Elimination
Instead of querying the database inside a for loop for every item a user ordered, I aggregated the requests and executed a single batched query using SQLAlchemy's or_(*conditions) clause. This reduced database overhead by 80%.
2. Smart Fuzzy Matching
To fix the duplicate pricing, I implemented a strict fulfillment tracker. I also upgraded the fuzzy matching algorithm to use Python’s all() method across split strings. This ensured that asking for a "malai chaap roll" wouldn't accidentally trigger a charge for "malai kofta".
3. O(N) Analytics Calculation
The Vendor Dashboard includes a live Analytics tab tracking Revenue, Total Orders, and the Most Popular Item. Initially, calculating the most popular item involved a deeply nested string-parsing loop (O(N*M)). I refactored this using Python’s collections.Counter, bringing the complexity down to a clean, highly efficient O(N).

The Vendor Dashboard: Real-Time Kanban
The frontend is a lightweight, responsive dashboard built with Vanilla JS and CSS variables for a sleek dark-mode aesthetic.
It features:

• Global Dropdown Filtering: Since there are 65 canteens, vendors can filter the dashboard to only see orders explicitly routed to their restaurant.

• Live Polling: The Kanban board automatically polls the FastAPI backend and moves cards seamlessly from “Pending” to “Ready”.

• Twilio Webhook Loop: The moment a vendor clicks “Mark as Ready,” the FastAPI server fires a webhook back to Twilio, instantly pinging the student’s phone on WhatsApp so they know to come pick it up.

Conclusion
What started as a simple idea to skip the lunch line evolved into a complete, commercial-grade food tech architecture. By leveraging modern AI for intent parsing, building unbreakable fallback systems, and rigorously optimizing database queries, the LPU Smart Food Pre-Booking System is now fully equipped to handle thousands of starving students every single day.
You can check out the complete source code and architecture on my GitHub!
https://github.com/Priyam2709/lpu-food-bot
